title: Pizza wheels are bad, Japanese toilets are great
gist: Smith uses the slow US adoption of pizza shears and Japanese washlets as an analogy for educated American progressives' dismissal of AI, arguing that whole social classes can reject objectively superior technology for status-protection reasons and risk being left behind by it.

insights.claim: Objectively superior consumer technologies can go un-adopted for decades by populations that would benefit, as shown by Americans sticking with pizza wheels over scissors/rocking cutters and ignoring Japanese washlets introduced in 1980.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Adoption lag is a real and persistent market dynamic; superior products do not automatically win share, so penetration curves can stay low for structural/cultural reasons, not just price.
insights.quote: There is no law of the Universe that useful technologies are adopted quickly by everyone who could make use of them.
insights.locator: This dismissiveness reminds me of the cases of the pizza wheel

insights.claim: Historically, nations that adopted cutting-edge technologies (gunpowder, industrial tech, computers) gained an edge over those that refused, and economic historians attribute refusal mainly to fear of disrupting existing power and elite status.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Frames technology-resistance as an elite status-protection behavior, a lens for spotting which incumbents/classes will block adoption of a disruptive tech.
insights.quote: a fear of disrupting existing patterns of power and elite status was behind the decision to eschew new technology

insights.claim: Anthropic is experiencing the fastest revenue growth of any large company in history and expects to turn an operating profit next quarter, contradicting the popular assumption that the AI buildout is a bursting bubble.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: If accurate, Anthropic's revenue trajectory and near-term operating profit undercut the AI-bubble thesis and support continued AI-infra and model-lab valuations.
insights.quote: Anthropic was experiencing the fastest revenue growth of any large company in history, and expects to turn an operating profit next quarter
insights.locator: When I told him that Anthropic

insights.claim: As recently as late 2025, AI data-center investment was still racing ahead of revenue and even Dario Amodei did not know whether Anthropic would go bankrupt, so the bubble fear had a real basis before the recent revenue inflection.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Marks late-2025 as the prior peak of AI-capex-vs-revenue anxiety; the subsequent revenue catch-up is the variable that flipped the narrative and is the one to watch.
insights.quote: as recently as late 2025 data center investment was still racing ahead of revenue and even Dario Amodei didn't know whether his company would go bankrupt
insights.locator: To be fair, not everyone pays close attention

insights.claim: Many observers assumed an AI bubble was definitely in progress and then never checked back to update on the data, leaving public perception lagging the actual industry fundamentals.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Persistent perception lag creates a sentiment-vs-fundamentals gap that can be a source of mispricing in AI-exposed assets.
insights.quote: lots of people seemed to have assumed that a bubble was definitely in progress, and then not bothered to check up on it later
insights.locator: But while observers close to the industry

insights.claim: Educated progressive professionals (lawyers, academics, artists) are systematically more dismissive of AI than the general public, with Democrats polling more negatively than Republicans on AI and on data-center construction.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: A partisan/class split in AI sentiment signals uneven adoption by region and profession, and predicts where political resistance to AI data-center buildout will concentrate.
insights.quote: Democrats consistently poll more negatively than Republicans, both on AI in general, and in terms of data center construction.

insights.claim: Americans overall are simultaneously fearful that AI will take their jobs and predict negative societal impacts, yet are increasingly using AI both at work and personally, per Pew data.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Revealed behavior (rising usage) diverges from stated sentiment (fear), so adoption metrics are a better demand signal than survey attitudes.
insights.quote: Americans have correctly identified the most useful technology, and are adopting it.
insights.locator: Unlike in the case of pizza cutters and washlets

insights.claim: By dismissing AI as a bubble, fancy autocomplete, IP theft, or slop, the left forecloses serious engagement with AI's real risks and opportunities, ceding influence over how the technology develops.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Indicates AI governance/safety debate will be shaped more by the right and industry than the left, with implications for the regulatory trajectory.
insights.quote: by thinking that the most important technological revolution of the modern age can be waved away as a "bubble" or "fancy autocomplete" or IP theft or slop
insights.locator: In fact, by dismissing AI's potential

insights.claim: The long era in which innovation disrupted blue-collar work but spared high-level white-collar work is probably ending, exposing educated professionals to AI-driven displacement for the first time.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Signals the next wave of automation risk shifts to high-wage knowledge work (law, academia, art), reshaping labor demand and the economics of professional-services firms.
insights.quote: educated professional types have had a long period of security, in which new innovations disrupted blue-collar work but not high-level white-collar work. That's probably over now.
